title: Reconfigurable chiral superconductivity
authors: Surajit Dutta, Nadav Auerbach, Tonghang Han, Yaozhang Zhou, Gal Shavit, Niladri-Sekhar Kander, Yuri Myasoedov, Martin E. Huber, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Long Ju, Eli Zeldov
abstract: Rhombohedral multilayer graphene at high displacement fields hosts superconductivity emerging from a spin valley polarized quarter metal, with transport signatures suggestive of time reversal symmetry (TRS) breaking and chiral superconductivity (CSC). These observations have motivated proposals of topological superconductivity and non-Abelian quasiparticles, yet direct magnetic evidence and microscopic insight into the superconducting state remain lacking, limiting understanding of this unique state. Here we use nanoscale SQUID on tip magnetometry to image isospin-polarized domains in rhombohedral pentalayer graphene and establish CSC via spatially resolved thermodynamic detection of TRS breaking. We find that the density at which domain walls proliferate at elevated temperatures coincides with the onset of CSC, indicating an underlying transition in the parent state that both induces superconductivity and reduces domain wall energy. We further show that the chiral domain structure in the superconducting phase is inherited from the isospin-polarized parent state. Strikingly, the CSC phase exhibits multiple transport regimes governed by configurations of chiral domains separated by highly resistive domain walls. We demonstrate deterministic, ultra low current control of these domains, enabling reversible switching between states of opposite chirality a defining CSC property absent in other superconductors. These results establish rhombohedral graphene as a unique platform for reconfigurable CSC and ultra low power electronic functionality based on controllable isospin textures.
summary: This paper provides direct magnetic evidence for chiral superconductivity in rhombohedral graphene and shows that the superconducting state inherits its domain structure from a parent isospin-polarized phase. It demonstrates that these chiral domains can be switched reversibly with extremely low currents, suggesting potential for ultra-low-power electronic functionality.
main_problem: Determining the origin of chiral superconductivity (CSC) in rhombohedral multilayer graphene and whether its time-reversal symmetry breaking is inherited from the underlying isospin-polarized parent state.
main_result: The study establishes that CSC in rhombohedral pentalayer graphene inherits its chiral domain structure from the isospin-polarized 1/4M phase and demonstrates that these domains can be deterministically reconfigured using ultra-low currents.
method: Nanoscale SQUID-on-tip (SOT) magnetometry is used for spatial imaging of magnetic textures, combined with transport measurements and current-driven manipulation of isospin domains.
model_or_system: Rhombohedral pentalayer graphene (R5G) at high displacement fields, characterized by a spin-valley-polarized quarter-metal (1/4M) phase and a superconducting phase.
key_observables: Out-of-plane magnetization (Mz), differential magnetization (mz), longitudinal resistance (Rxx), Hall resistance (Rxy), and stray field (Bz,ac).
important_parameters: Temperature (down to 20 mK), carrier density (n), displacement field (D), and current-driven switching at the nanoampere scale.
main_assumptions: The switching mechanism is assumed to be driven by linear momentum transfer from reflected electrons rather than spin-transfer torque.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows device geometry and gate control; Figure 2 illustrates transport hysteresis and the identification of domain walls; Figure 3 details the transition from bubble to system-spanning domain walls; Figure 4 and 5 demonstrate current-driven domain expansion, contraction, and resistance switching.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the R5G system, uses SOT imaging to prove TRS breaking in the CSC phase, demonstrates the inheritance of order from the 1/4M phase, analyzes the transport regimes (LR vs HR) caused by domain walls, and concludes with the demonstration of reversible, current-controlled domain reconfiguration.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
